title: Istio, Consul, and Service Mesh
description: A deep-dive mindmap of service mesh goals, how Istio and Consul approach them, data plane internals, security model, observability, and decision guidance.

root.label: Service Mesh
root.notes: A service mesh adds a control plane and data plane layer for service-to-service communication. The control plane distributes policy; the data plane (proxies) enforces it. Key goals: zero-trust security, consistent observability, fine-grained traffic control — all without changing application code.

children.label: Problems It Solves
children.notes: Service meshes remove duplicated networking logic from each service and centralise platform concerns. Each team no longer needs to implement mTLS, retries, or tracing individually.

children.label: Security
children.notes: Zero-trust east-west security via mutual TLS. Every workload gets a cryptographic identity (usually SPIFFE/SPIRE x509 SVID). AuthorizationPolicy rules enforce which services can talk to each other at L4 (IP/port) and L7 (HTTP method, path, JWT claims).

children.label: Mutual TLS (mTLS)
children.notes: Both the client and server present certificates, proving identity in both directions. The mesh handles certificate rotation automatically, typically every 24 hours, without restarting workloads.

children.label: SPIFFE / SPIRE Identity
children.notes: SPIFFE (Secure Production Identity Framework for Everyone) defines the standard. Each workload gets a SPIFFE ID like spiffe://cluster.local/ns/default/sa/checkout. The mesh CA (Istio Citadel / Consul CA) mints short-lived x509 SVIDs and distributes them via xDS or gRPC.

children.label: Authorization Policy
children.notes: Policy can allow or deny based on source principal (SPIFFE ID), namespace, HTTP method, path, headers, JWT claims, or IP ranges. Default-deny namespaces lock down blast radius of a compromised workload.

children.label: Traffic Management
children.notes: The mesh gives platform teams fine-grained control without touching application code.

children.label: Retries & Timeouts
children.notes: Configurable retry budgets (number of retries, retry-on conditions like 5xx or reset), per-request timeouts, and per-attempt timeouts. Prevents cascading failures by bounding how long a request can block.

children.label: Circuit Breaking
children.notes: Envoy's outlier detection ejects endpoints from a cluster when they exceed a failure threshold. Prevents sending traffic to known-bad backends. Configurable: consecutive 5xx count, detection interval, ejection percentage.

children.label: Traffic Splitting & Canary
children.notes: Weight-based routing splits a percentage of traffic between stable and canary versions. Header-based or metadata-based routing enables dark launches and A/B testing. Useful for progressive delivery alongside Argo Rollouts or Flagger.

children.label: Fault Injection
children.notes: Deliberately inject delays or abort HTTP requests at a configurable percentage. Used for chaos/resilience testing without external tooling. Only works in the Istio data path, so it tests realistic failure modes.

children.label: Observability
children.notes: The mesh emits a consistent telemetry baseline across all services — no SDK required.

children.label: Metrics (Prometheus)
children.notes: Envoy exposes standard HTTP metrics per service pair: request count, error rate, latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99). Scraped by Prometheus; visualised in Grafana dashboards shipped with Istio (istio-workload, istio-service).

children.label: Distributed Tracing
children.notes: Envoy propagates B3 or W3C trace context headers between services. Traces are sent to Zipkin, Jaeger, or an OTLP-compatible backend. Apps only need to forward the trace headers, not generate spans.

children.label: Access Logs
children.notes: Per-request structured logs from every proxy, including upstream/downstream identity, latency, bytes, and response code. Can be streamed via gRPC Access Log Service (ALS) to a central collector.

children.label: Kiali (Service Graph)
children.notes: Kiali consumes Prometheus metrics and Istio config to render a real-time service topology graph with health, traffic rates, and mTLS status per edge. Useful for understanding blast radius and validating policy.

children.label: Platform Governance
children.notes: Platform teams set defaults (mTLS mode, tracing sample rate, rate limits). App teams inherit them without boilerplate. Teams can opt into additional policies (AuthorizationPolicy, destination rules) via namespace-scoped CRDs.

children.label: PeerAuthentication (mTLS mode)
children.notes: STRICT enforces mTLS for all connections in a namespace. PERMISSIVE allows both plaintext and mTLS — useful during migration. DISABLE turns off mTLS. Applied per namespace or per workload selector.

children.label: Multi-tenancy
children.notes: Namespace isolation via AuthorizationPolicy. RBAC on Istio CRDs via Kubernetes RBAC. Platform team owns mesh-wide PeerAuthentication; app teams own their own AuthorizationPolicy and VirtualService within their namespace.

children.label: Data Plane: Envoy
children.notes: Both Istio and Consul use Envoy as their default data plane proxy. Envoy is a high-performance C++ proxy designed for L7 workloads. It receives its configuration dynamically via xDS APIs from the control plane.

children.label: xDS API
children.notes: xDS (discovery service) is the protocol Envoy uses to fetch config from a control plane. Key APIs: LDS (listeners), RDS (routes), CDS (clusters/upstreams), EDS (endpoints). Istio istiod and Consul serve xDS to Envoy sidecars or ztunnel.

children.label: Listeners & Filter Chains
children.notes: Envoy listens on ports and applies filter chains. L4 filters handle TCP/TLS; L7 filters (HTTP connection manager, gRPC stats, JWT authn, ext_authz) handle HTTP. The mesh control plane programs these automatically.

children.label: Clusters & Load Balancing
children.notes: Each upstream service is an Envoy cluster. Load balancing policies include round-robin, least-request, ring hash (sticky sessions), and random. Outlier detection runs per-cluster for circuit breaking.

children.label: Wasm / Custom Filters
children.notes: Envoy supports WebAssembly extensions for custom filters. Istio's WasmPlugin CRD distributes Wasm filters to sidecars or waypoints. Useful for custom auth, rate limiting, or header manipulation without recompiling Envoy.

children.label: Istio
children.notes: Istio is a feature-rich service mesh built around policy, telemetry, and traffic control. It integrates deeply with Kubernetes and Envoy. Its newer ambient mode reduces per-pod overhead significantly.

children.label: Control Plane: istiod
children.notes: Istiod is the unified control plane. It bundles three historical components: Pilot (xDS config generation), Citadel (CA and certificate management), and Galley (config validation). It connects to the Kubernetes API to watch Services, Pods, and Istio CRDs.

children.label: Pilot (Config Distribution)
children.notes: Pilot watches Kubernetes resources and Istio CRDs, then generates Envoy xDS config (LDS/RDS/CDS/EDS) and pushes it to all proxies. It abstracts Kubernetes service discovery into Envoy cluster membership.

children.label: Citadel (Certificate Authority)
children.notes: Citadel is the mesh CA. It signs x509 SVIDs for workloads using their Kubernetes ServiceAccount as identity. Certificates are short-lived (default 24h) and rotated automatically. It can integrate with external CAs (Vault, AWS ACM PCA) via the pluggable CA interface.

children.label: Galley (Config Validation)
children.notes: Galley validates Istio CRD resources before they are accepted. It prevents misconfigured VirtualService or DestinationRule from being applied. The admission webhook runs in istiod in modern versions.

children.label: Key CRDs
children.notes: Istio extends Kubernetes with CRDs for traffic management, security, and configuration.

children.label: VirtualService
children.notes: Configures how requests are routed to a service. Supports weight-based splitting, header matching, fault injection, retries, and timeouts. Operates at L7. Example: route 10% of /api traffic to v2 of checkout service.

children.label: DestinationRule
children.notes: Configures traffic policy after routing decision: load balancing algorithm, connection pool limits, circuit breaker thresholds, TLS mode for upstream connections, and subset definitions (used by VirtualService).

children.label: Gateway (Ingress/Egress)
children.notes: Configures an Istio Gateway proxy for north-south traffic. Replaces Kubernetes Ingress with richer TLS, SNI routing, and traffic management features. Egress Gateway controls outbound traffic to external services.

children.label: ServiceEntry
children.notes: Registers external services (outside the mesh) into the service registry so Istio can apply policy, TLS origination, and observability to egress traffic. Example: make api.stripe.com visible to mesh policy.

children.label: AuthorizationPolicy
children.notes: Allow/deny rules based on source principal (SPIFFE), namespace, HTTP attributes, or JWT claims. Applied per workload or namespace. Default-deny means nothing is allowed until explicitly permitted.

children.label: PeerAuthentication
children.notes: Sets mTLS mode (STRICT, PERMISSIVE, DISABLE) for inbound connections. Usually set STRICT at namespace level by platform team and relaxed selectively during migration.

children.label: Classic Sidecar Mode
children.notes: Each workload pod gets an injected Envoy sidecar (istio-proxy container). init containers rewrite iptables rules to intercept all traffic through the proxy. Provides strong per-workload isolation and full L7 inspection but increases resource usage (CPU + memory) and slows pod startup.

children.label: Sidecar Injection
children.notes: Automatic injection via MutatingAdmissionWebhook when namespace is labelled `istio-injection: enabled`. Manual injection with `istioctl kube-inject`. The injected proxy runs as a separate container sharing the pod network namespace.

children.label: iptables Traffic Interception
children.notes: istio-init (or CNI plugin) adds iptables rules to redirect all inbound/outbound traffic through the Envoy proxy on port 15001/15006. Applications are unaware; the proxy transparently intercepts all TCP connections.

children.label: Ambient Mesh (newer mode)
children.notes: Ambient mesh removes per-pod sidecars for most workloads by splitting data plane duties into two layers: a shared node-level L4 proxy (ztunnel) and optional namespace/service-scoped L7 proxies (waypoints). Reduces CPU, memory, and operational complexity significantly.

children.label: ztunnel (L4)
children.notes: A lightweight Rust-based L4 proxy that runs as a DaemonSet on every node. Handles mTLS establishment, SPIFFE identity enforcement, and basic L4 authorization for all workloads on the node. Uses HBONE (HTTP-based overlay network encapsulation) to tunnel traffic between nodes. Much lower overhead than per-pod Envoy.

children.label: Waypoints (L7)
children.notes: Full Envoy proxies deployed only where L7 policy is needed. A waypoint is scoped to a Kubernetes Gateway resource (using Gateway API). When a namespace or service has a waypoint, ztunnel forwards traffic through it for L7 inspection, AuthorizationPolicy enforcement, and HTTP routing. Workloads without L7 policy needs skip waypoints entirely.

children.label: HBONE Tunnelling
children.notes: HBONE (HTTP Based Overlay Network Environment) is the tunnel protocol used between ztunnels and between ztunnel and waypoints. It uses HTTP/2 CONNECT over mTLS to carry inner TCP streams. This lets the L4 mesh work across arbitrary networks without needing a CNI overlay change.

children.label: Ambient vs Sidecar Trade-offs
children.notes: Ambient: lower resource overhead, no init container, no iptables per pod, easier rollout. Sidecars: stronger workload isolation (each pod has its own proxy process), supports all L7 features per-workload without waypoints. Mixed mode is supported: some namespaces can use sidecars and others ambient in the same cluster.

children.label: Ingress & Egress
children.notes: Istio manages both north-south (external-to-cluster) and south-north (cluster-to-external) traffic through dedicated gateway deployments.

children.label: Ingress Gateway
children.notes: A dedicated Envoy deployment at the cluster edge. Handles TLS termination, SNI-based routing, HTTP→HTTPS redirect, and passes traffic into the mesh. Replaces Nginx/Traefik for mesh-aware deployments. Supports Kubernetes Gateway API as well as Istio's own Gateway CRD.

children.label: Egress Gateway
children.notes: Routes all outbound traffic through a controlled exit point. Enables auditing, policy enforcement, and TLS origination for external calls. Important for regulated environments that require all external traffic to pass through a known IP/gateway.

children.label: Strengths
children.notes: Very strong traffic policy, rich telemetry out-of-the-box, mature RBAC/policy model, Kubernetes-native, active CNCF project, clear sidecar-to-ambient migration path.

children.label: Best For
children.notes: Kubernetes-only or Kubernetes-primary deployments. Teams that need deep L7 policy, progressive delivery, or rich observability. Organizations that want a fully open-source CNCF-graduated mesh.

children.label: Weaknesses / Gotchas
children.notes: Steep learning curve for CRDs. Sidecar mode has non-trivial resource overhead at scale. iptables interception can interfere with host-network pods. Ambient mode is newer and some advanced features (e.g., Wasm to waypoints) are still maturing.

children.label: Upgrade Complexity
children.notes: Istio upgrades require care: canary upgrades using revision tags reduce risk. istiod versions and sidecar versions must stay in sync (skew tolerance is ±1 minor version). Ambient mode simplifies upgrades since ztunnel is separate from workloads.

children.label: Consul
children.notes: Consul is HashiCorp's service networking platform. It combines service discovery, health checking, KV store, and service mesh (Consul Connect) in one product. Strong multi-datacenter and multi-runtime story (VMs, K8s, Lambda, ECS).

children.label: Architecture
children.notes: Consul has servers (raft-based cluster, 3 or 5 nodes) and clients (lightweight agent on every node). Clients register services, forward queries to servers, and run health checks locally. The Consul Dataplane model removes client agents in Kubernetes environments.

children.label: Consul Servers
children.notes: 3 or 5 server nodes form a Raft cluster for consistency. They hold the service catalog, KV store, ACL tokens, intentions, and CA state. Leader election happens automatically. Cross-DC communication uses WAN gossip pool.

children.label: Consul Clients / Agents
children.notes: Lightweight daemons on each host. They forward RPC to servers, run local health checks, and participate in LAN gossip for failure detection. In newer Kubernetes deployments, the Consul Dataplane replaces client agents with a lighter per-pod connection.

children.label: Consul Dataplane (K8s)
children.notes: In Kubernetes, Consul Dataplane replaces the full Consul client agent with a smaller component that runs alongside Envoy sidecars. It handles xDS proxying between Envoy and Consul servers. Reduces resource overhead and simplifies the daemonset model.

children.label: Service Discovery & Health
children.notes: Core Consul feature predating service mesh. Services register with Consul (agent or API). Consul performs health checks (HTTP, TCP, script, gRPC) and removes unhealthy instances from DNS/API responses. DNS interface at .consul domain; HTTP API for programmatic access.

children.label: DNS Interface
children.notes: Services resolve as <service>.service.consul or <service>.service.<dc>.consul. Supports A records (IPs), SRV records (port), and prepared queries for advanced filtering. Integrates with CoreDNS in Kubernetes via a stub zone.

children.label: Health Checks
children.notes: Multiple check types: HTTP (GET endpoint must return 2xx), TCP (port open), TTL (app must heartbeat), script (arbitrary command), gRPC, and Docker exec. Checks run locally on the agent for low-latency failure detection.

children.label: Consul Connect (Service Mesh)
children.notes: Consul Connect adds mTLS, service identity, and traffic policy to Consul. It uses Envoy as the data plane proxy (via Consul Dataplane or client agent). Services get identities from the Consul CA and communicate through authenticated sidecars.

children.label: Intentions (Authorization)
children.notes: Intentions are Consul's allow/deny authorization model between services. They can be coarse-grained (source service → dest service: allow/deny) or fine-grained (with HTTP method/path matching in L7 intentions). Map loosely to Istio AuthorizationPolicy.

children.label: Transparent Proxy / Connect
children.notes: Consul Connect uses iptables (via init container or CNI) to intercept all pod traffic through the Envoy sidecar. Provides mTLS and identity enforcement. This is the primary Consul mesh data path for Kubernetes.

children.label: Service Config Entries
children.notes: CRD-like Consul config entries: ServiceDefaults (protocol, mesh gateway mode), ServiceResolver (failover, load balancing), ServiceRouter (L7 routing rules), ServiceSplitter (traffic splitting). Equivalent to Istio VirtualService + DestinationRule.

children.label: Gateways
children.notes: Consul supports several gateway types for controlling traffic at boundaries.

children.label: Mesh Gateway
children.notes: Routes service mesh traffic between Consul datacenters or Kubernetes clusters. Essential for multi-DC federation. The mesh gateway terminates the outer mTLS and re-establishes it to the destination.

children.label: Ingress Gateway
children.notes: Allows external clients (outside the mesh) to connect to mesh services. Similar to Istio ingress gateway but configured via IngressGateway config entry and Consul listener rules.

children.label: Terminating Gateway
children.notes: Handles mesh-to-external-service traffic. Terminates mTLS on the mesh side and connects to external services (databases, legacy APIs) that are not in the mesh. Equivalent to Istio's ServiceEntry + Egress Gateway pattern.

children.label: API Gateway (L7)
children.notes: HashiCorp Consul API Gateway provides L7 HTTP routing for ingress. It supports JWT auth, rate limiting, and request routing based on headers and paths — similar to an Istio Gateway with VirtualService. The nearest analogue to waypoint-style shared L7 enforcement.

children.label: Multi-DC & Multi-Runtime
children.notes: Consul's strongest differentiator vs Istio: native multi-datacenter federation, WAN gossip, and support for non-Kubernetes runtimes (VMs, ECS, Lambda, bare metal). Services across DCs can discover and connect to each other through mesh gateways.

children.label: No Ambient-Mode Equivalent
children.notes: Consul does not have a 1:1 ambient mesh equivalent. The closest is transparent proxy + Consul Dataplane, which still relies on per-workload Envoy sidecars rather than a shared node-level L4 proxy. HashiCorp has not announced a ztunnel-style sidecarless architecture.

children.label: HCP Consul (Managed)
children.notes: HashiCorp Cloud Platform offers managed Consul servers. Removes the operational burden of running Consul server clusters. Clients (or Consul Dataplane) on-premises or in K8s connect to HCP-managed servers. Good for teams that want Consul's features without self-managing Raft clusters.

children.label: ACL System
children.notes: Consul uses a token-based ACL system to authorize access to its API, KV, and service mesh features. ACL policies define what a token can read/write. ACL roles group policies. Tokens can be bound to Kubernetes service accounts via auth methods (Consul's equivalent of IRSA/Workload Identity).

children.label: Key-Value Store
children.notes: Consul includes a distributed KV store often used for feature flags, dynamic configuration, and service configuration. Not typically used in service mesh scenarios but frequently used alongside it in HashiCorp-heavy stacks (Vault + Consul + Nomad).

children.label: Strengths
children.notes: Best multi-datacenter story. Works outside Kubernetes (VMs, ECS, Nomad). Simpler operational model for heterogeneous infrastructure. Integrated service catalog and health checking before adding mesh.

children.label: Best For
children.notes: Multi-DC or hybrid cloud deployments. Organizations that also run workloads on VMs or ECS. Teams already using HashiCorp stack (Vault, Nomad, Terraform). When service discovery and health checking are needed independent of mesh.

children.label: Weaknesses / Gotchas
children.notes: Lighter L7 traffic management than Istio in open-source. Intentions model is simpler but less expressive than Istio AuthorizationPolicy. No ambient-mode equivalent. Enterprise features (advanced federation, audit logging, OIDC namespace isolation) require paid license.

children.label: OSS vs Enterprise Gap
children.notes: Some important features (namespace isolation, audit logging, OIDC provider, read replicas, network segments) are in Consul Enterprise or HCP Consul, not OSS. Be aware when evaluating total cost.

children.label: Concept Mapping
children.notes: Practical equivalents across Istio and Consul. Not perfect matches — the operational models differ significantly.

children.label: ztunnel ↔ Consul Transparent Proxy
children.notes: Both handle node/pod-level L4 interception with mTLS and identity. ztunnel is a shared node-level DaemonSet (lower overhead). Consul transparent proxy is per-sidecar in a pod. Consul has no node-level shared L4 proxy equivalent.

children.label: Waypoints ↔ Consul API Gateway / Terminating GW
children.notes: Waypoints are shared L7 policy enforcement points deployed per namespace or service. Consul's nearest fit is API Gateway (ingress L7) or dedicated proxy-based enforcement at boundaries.

children.label: VirtualService ↔ ServiceRouter + ServiceSplitter
children.notes: Istio VirtualService maps to Consul's ServiceRouter (L7 routing rules) and ServiceSplitter (traffic splitting) config entries. Istio's API is more expressive for complex routing; Consul's is simpler to get started.

children.label: DestinationRule ↔ ServiceResolver
children.notes: Istio DestinationRule (subsets, load balancing, circuit breaker) maps to Consul ServiceResolver (failover, redirect, load balancer config) and ServiceDefaults (connection limits).

children.label: AuthorizationPolicy ↔ Intentions
children.notes: Both enforce allow/deny between services. Istio AuthorizationPolicy is richer: JWT claims, HTTP method/path, namespace, source principal. Consul Intentions are simpler: service-to-service allow/deny, plus L7 intentions for HTTP method/path matching in enterprise.

children.label: Citadel (Istio CA) ↔ Consul CA
children.notes: Both issue short-lived x509 SVIDs to workloads for mTLS identity. Istio Citadel uses Kubernetes ServiceAccount JWTs as proof-of-identity. Consul CA uses Consul auth methods (K8s service account, Vault) to issue certificates. Both support plugging in an external CA (Vault).

children.label: ServiceEntry + Egress GW ↔ Terminating Gateway
children.notes: For mesh-to-external-service traffic: Istio uses ServiceEntry to register the external service and optionally routes through an Egress Gateway. Consul uses a Terminating Gateway to proxy outbound connections to services outside the mesh.

children.label: Ambient Mesh (no sidecar) ↔ No Consul equivalent
children.notes: Istio ambient mesh removes most sidecars via ztunnel + waypoints. Consul still requires per-workload Envoy sidecars in its service mesh model. This is a meaningful architectural gap when resource efficiency is a priority.

children.label: Decision Guide
children.notes: Use this branch to reason about which mesh (or no mesh) is right for a given context.

children.label: Pick Istio When…
children.notes: You are Kubernetes-only or primarily Kubernetes. You need deep L7 traffic management (VirtualService, fault injection, progressive delivery with Argo Rollouts/Flagger). You want the richest CNCF-graduated open-source mesh. You care about ambient mode for resource efficiency at scale.

children.label: Pick Consul When…
children.notes: You have a hybrid environment (Kubernetes + VMs, ECS, Nomad, bare metal). You need multi-datacenter service mesh with WAN federation. You are already using the HashiCorp stack (Vault for secrets, Nomad for scheduling). Simpler intentions-based policy is sufficient.

children.label: Use Both?
children.notes: Rare, but possible: use Consul for multi-DC service discovery and health checking across all runtimes, and Istio for fine-grained Kubernetes-only L7 mesh policy. Complexity is high; usually one or the other is sufficient.

children.label: Skip the Mesh When…
children.notes: You have fewer than ~10 services, no strict east-west security requirement, and your cloud provider's native load balancer and IAM are sufficient. Meshes add operational overhead — don't adopt one without a clear problem to solve.

children.label: Kubernetes Gateway API
children.notes: Both Istio and Consul are moving toward the Kubernetes Gateway API (io.k8s.gateway.networking) as the standard ingress/mesh API, replacing their proprietary CRDs for L7 routing. Prefer Gateway API resources in new deployments for portability.

children.label: What It Does Not Solve
children.notes: Service meshes help with service-to-service networking but do not remove broader distributed-systems or operational complexity.

children.label: Multi-cloud Complexity
children.notes: A mesh does not make multi-cloud easy. Different clouds still bring separate networking, DNS, identity, load balancers, firewall rules, and failure modes. The mesh helps with east-west mTLS but not with cloud-level IAM or network topologies.

children.label: Application Design Flaws
children.notes: It does not fix idempotency, data consistency, saga design, poor retry semantics, or N+1 database queries. Retries at the mesh level can amplify bugs if applications are not idempotent.

children.label: Operational Complexity
children.notes: You still need platform expertise, CRD lifecycle management, policy governance, observability tuning, certificate CA operations, and careful rollout/upgrade governance. A mesh adds a new control plane that must be operated.

children.label: Not a Replacement for Network Segmentation
children.notes: mTLS and AuthorizationPolicy protect service-to-service traffic, but they do not replace Kubernetes NetworkPolicy, cloud security groups, or host-level firewall rules. Defense-in-depth means using all layers.

children.label: Mesh ≠ API Gateway
children.notes: A service mesh focuses on east-west (service-to-service) traffic. It is not a full API gateway for north-south (external-to-internal) traffic management, developer portal, or API monetization. Use a dedicated API gateway (Kong, NGINX, Apigee) for that layer.
